content: GBS treatment includes immunotherapy and intensive supportive care. Immunotherapy (initiate within 2 weeks of onset): IVIG (0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days) or plasma exchange (5 exchanges over 1-2 weeks)—equally effective; combining provides no additional benefit. Steroids alone are NOT effective in GBS. Supportive care is critical: ICU monitoring for respiratory failure (FVC <20 mL/kg or NIF <-30 cmH2O indicates need for intubation), autonomic instability (continuous cardiac monitoring, avoid sudden position changes), DVT prophylaxis, pain management (gabapentin, carbamazepine, opioids), nutrition (early NG tube if bulbar involvement), physical/occupational therapy. Complications: aspiration pneumonia, PE, pressure ulcers, ileus, urinary retention. Prognosis: 80% walk independently at 6 months, 60% full recovery at 1 year; 20% have persistent disability. Poor prognostic factors: older age, rapid progression, need for ventilation, hyponatremia, axonal variants. Chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP) is the chronic counterpart (>8 weeks progression) requiring long-term immunotherapy.
